analysis_summary: The proposed method produced 9 finding(s) from 10 analysis obligation(s).

findings.summary: Visible top-level RTL connects DMI/JTAG debug requests into dm_top without an explicit top-level jtag_unlock/debug authorization gate. dm_master memory-master responses are tied off, reducing memory-master impact, but debug control outputs remain reachable if dmi_jtag itself does not enforce authorization.
findings.vulnerability_category: issue DMI read/write operations, debug requests, and debug reset/control effects / dm_top debug module controls including ndmreset_o, dmactive_o, debug_req_o, dm_slave_* register path, and disabled dm_master_* path / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: external JTAG/DTM debug requester via tck_i/tms_i/trst_ni/td_i or simulation DTM/JTAG Operation: issue DMI read/write operations, debug requests, and debug reset/control effects Object: dm_top debug module controls including ndmreset_o, dmactive_o, debug_req_o, dm_slave_* register path, and disabled dm_master_* path Expected guard: DMI/JTAG operations reaching protected debug controls should be gated by explicit authorization such as jtag_unlock, authenticated DMI state, lifecycle/testmode restriction, or debug_mode_i policy before dm_top control outputs are driven. Observed behavior: dmi_jtag receives hash inputs and outputs jtag_unlock, but debug_req/debug_req_valid are visibly connected directly from i_dmi_jtag, or SimDTM in simulation, into dm_top. The provided evidence does not show jtag_unlock gating dmi_req_valid_i, dmi_req_i, ndmreset_o, dmactive_o, or debug_req_o. dm_master_gnt, dm_master_r_valid, and dm_master_r_rdata are tied to zero. Path: JTAG pins -> dmi_jtag -> debug_req/debug_req_valid -> dm_top -> ndmreset_o/dmactive_o/debug_req_o and dm_slave path; simulation path SimDTM -> debug_req/debug_req_valid -> dm_top. Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Unauthenticated or insufficiently authenticated JTAG/DTM may activate debug, request hart debug entry, or drive debug reset/control if dmi_jtag does not internally enforce authorization.

findings.summary: Boot ROM request enable is access-control gated, but the response data path is visibly not gated by acc_ctrl, leaving possible stale/combinational ROM data exposure if downstream blocks return data despite denied req_i.
findings.vulnerability_category: read boot ROM through NoC AXI-lite path / bootrom/bootrom_linux contents and rom_rdata response / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: NoC/AXI peripheral requester with priv_lvl_i Operation: read boot ROM through NoC AXI-lite path Object: bootrom/bootrom_linux contents and rom_rdata response Expected guard: Boot ROM reads should be permitted only when acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][0] grants access; denied reads should not expose ROM data or stale ROM response data. Observed behavior: rom_req = rom_req_acct && acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][0], and bootrom instances receive rom_req. However, axi2mem consumes rom_rdata directly, and rom_rdata is assigned from rom_rdata_linux without an explicit acc_ctrl gate. ariane_boot_sel_i mux arms both select rom_rdata_linux. Path: buf_ariane_bootrom_noc2_* -> noc_axilite_bridge -> br_master -> axi2mem -> rom_req_acct/rom_addr -> rom_req gate -> bootrom_linux -> rom_rdata -> axi2mem/br_master response -> ariane_bootrom_buf_noc3_* Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Denied boot ROM reads may receive stale or unguarded rom_rdata if axi2mem/bootrom response behavior is not coupled to rom_req denial.

findings.summary: Top-level shows ACCT and REGLK wrappers are present and receive access-control/lock inputs, but wrapper internals were not provided, so write privilege, sticky lock, reset default, and jtag_unlock bypass semantics cannot be confirmed.
findings.vulnerability_category: configure or overwrite access-control and register-lock policy state / acc_ctrl privilege/access matrix and reglk_ctrl lock state / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: software-visible NoC/AXI requester to ACCT and REGLK NoC ports Operation: configure or overwrite access-control and register-lock policy state Object: acc_ctrl privilege/access matrix and reglk_ctrl lock state Expected guard: Policy register writes should be privilege/access controlled and lock protected; lock/configuration state should not be clearable except by authorized reset/lifecycle transition. Observed behavior: acct_wrapper receives reglk_ctrl_i, acct_ctrl_i, acc_ctrl_o, we_flag_0, and rst_6. reglk_wrapper receives reglk_ctrl_i, acct_ctrl_i, jtag_unlock, and rst_9 and emits reglk_ctrl_o. acc_ctrl_c is derived from acc_ctrl and used by peripheral gates. No wrapper write-enable/reset/sticky-bit logic is included in the provided evidence. Path: NoC ACCT/REGLK ports -> noc_axilite_bridge -> acct_wrapper/reglk_wrapper -> acc_ctrl_o/reglk_ctrl_o -> acc_ctrl_c and peripheral wrapper policy inputs Closure verdict: Related RTL evidence exists, but some core evidence slots remain incomplete.
findings.security_impact: If ACCT/REGLK wrapper write paths are not properly privileged/sticky, policy bits protecting many peripherals could be overwritten or cleared.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: acct_wrapper and reglk_wrapper source internals, reset defaults, lock clear behavior, and jtag_unlock effect.

findings.summary: DMA wrapper gates decoded transactions with acct_ctrl_i but does not visibly use reglk_ctrl_i for configuration/start writes. core_lock semantics appear insufficiently evidenced and rst_8 clears DMA registers, allowing software reset to clear protected DMA configuration.
findings.vulnerability_category: write DMA configuration and trigger transfer / DMA start, length, source_addr, dest_addr, done/end, and core_lock configuration registers / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: AXI/NoC requester configuring DMA Operation: write DMA configuration and trigger transfer Object: DMA start, length, source_addr, dest_addr, done/end, and core_lock configuration registers Expected guard: DMA configuration and start writes should require acct_ctrl_i and lock permission from reglk_ctrl_i/core_lock; start should not bypass locked configuration or privilege policy. Observed behavior: dma_wrapper assigns en = en_acct && acct_ctrl_i, providing account/privilege gating. reglk_ctrl_i is declared but no use is visible in the wrapper evidence. Reset condition ~(rst_ni && ~rst_8) clears core_lock_reg/start/length/source/dest registers. Top-level passes reglk_ctrl[8*8+7:8*8], acct_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][8], we_flag_3, pmpcfg_i, pmpaddr_i, and rst_8 into dma_wrapper. Path: buf_ariane_dma_noc2_* -> noc_axilite_bridge -> dma_axi_req -> dma_wrapper axi_lite_interface -> en_acct -> en/acct_ctrl_i -> DMA config registers -> dma instance start_i/length/source/dest -> valid_o Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: An authorized-at-current-privilege requester may be able to modify locked DMA configuration because reglk_ctrl_i is not visibly enforced. A reset path may clear core_lock/configuration and enable reconfiguration.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Full write decode after line 124 and exact core_lock use are truncated; candidate based on visible absence of reglk_ctrl_i use.

findings.summary: DMA includes PMP checks for source reads and destination writes, but visible sequencing contains bypass/stale-authorization hazards: a we_flag/length special-case can force source pmp_allow_new, and pmp_allow_reg is reset to 1 after load before destination checks.
findings.vulnerability_category: read source and write destination DMA transfer / memory regions reachable through configured source/destination addresses / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: DMA engine as bus master Operation: read source and write destination DMA transfer Object: memory regions reachable through configured source/destination addresses Expected guard: Each current source and destination address should be checked against PMP for the correct access type before transfer; stale pmp_allow_reg or special flags must not authorize changed addresses/parameters. Observed behavior: CTRL_CHECK_LOAD iterates source addresses with ACCESS_READ, but pmp_allow_new includes an override `(we_flag && length_d == 3)`. CTRL_LOAD asserts valid load, then sets pmp_check_ctr_new = length_d, pmp_addr_new = 0, and pmp_allow_new = 1 before store checks. CTRL_CHECK_STORE checks destination addresses with ACCESS_WRITE but sets pmp_allow_new = pmp_data_allow, not an accumulated allow across all beats, and final transition depends on pmp_allow_reg && pmp_data_allow. Path: DMA config registers -> dma save_inputs -> pmp_addr_reg/pmp_access_type_reg -> pmp pmp_data_allow -> pmp_allow_reg -> valid_reg/valid_o Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Special-case override or non-accumulated destination checks may permit DMA transfer signaling despite a denied beat, stale pmp_allow state, or policy bypass under we_flag/length_d==3.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Full FSM initialization, abort behavior, pmp instantiation wiring, and valid_o consumer semantics are truncated.

findings.summary: PMP allow priority/no-match logic is mostly architectural, but pmp_entry NAPOT/NA4 matching appears incorrect: mask is computed as `'1 << size` and base uses bitwise AND with that mask, which does not form a range mask and can mis-match addresses.
findings.vulnerability_category: authorize memory access based on PMP entry match / PMP-protected memory ranges / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: memory access requester using PMP check Operation: authorize memory access based on PMP entry match Object: PMP-protected memory ranges Expected guard: PMP matching should correctly implement OFF/TOR/NA4/NAPOT ranges, first-match priority, access-type/privilege semantics, and default deny for non-M no-match. Observed behavior: pmp.sv initializes allow_o=0, iterates entries in order, applies entries in S/U or locked M mode, checks `(access_type_i & conf_i[i].access_type) == access_type_i`, breaks on first match, allows M-mode no-match, and denies non-M no-match. pmp_entry implements TOR as prev <= addr < current. For NA4/NAPOT it computes size, then `mask = '1 << size`, `base = (conf_addr_i << 2) & mask`, and matches `(addr_i & mask) == base`, which appears to test only one shifted bit rather than a full NAPOT/NA4 range mask. Path: addr_i/access_type_i/priv_lvl_i/conf_i/conf_addr_i -> pmp_entry match_o -> pmp first-match allow_o Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Incorrect NA4/NAPOT matching could grant or deny accesses outside/inside configured PMP ranges, undermining DMA/core memory protection.

findings.summary: Provided evidence shows the generic axi_lite_interface performs no privilege checking and returns OK responses, but CLINT top-level integration/register module evidence is insufficient to determine whether riscv_peripherals applies acc_ctrl to CLINT writes.
findings.vulnerability_category: write CLINT MMIO registers controlling timer/software interrupts / CLINT timer compare/software interrupt state driving timer_irq_o and ipi_o / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: AXI/NoC requester to CLINT Operation: write CLINT MMIO registers controlling timer/software interrupts Object: CLINT timer compare/software interrupt state driving timer_irq_o and ipi_o Expected guard: CLINT writes should be gated by address decode and privilege/access-control policy so unauthorized requesters cannot alter timer/IPI state. Observed behavior: axi_lite_interface accepts AXI-lite requests and emits address_o, en_o, we_o, data_o without a privilege input; it passes data_i to read response and sets AXI response status to OK. Top-level ports expose buf_ariane_clint_noc* and timer_irq_o/ipi_o, but the CLINT wrapper/register logic and any acc_ctrl_c gate for CLINT are not included in the observations. Path: buf_ariane_clint_noc2_* -> CLINT NoC/AXI-lite path -> axi_lite_interface address_o/en_o/we_o/data_o -> CLINT registers -> timer_irq_o/ipi_o Closure verdict: Related RTL evidence exists, but some core evidence slots remain incomplete.
findings.security_impact: If top-level CLINT integration lacks acc_ctrl gating, any NoC requester reaching CLINT could raise IPIs or alter timer interrupts. Evidence is insufficient to assert this for the integrated design.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: CLINT integration around riscv_peripherals and CLINT register module/write gates.

findings.summary: Reset-control NoC interface and per-peripheral reset outputs are present, and DMA rst_8 clears DMA configuration/core_lock, but reset wrapper write privilege and whether protected policy state can be cleared are not evidenced.
findings.vulnerability_category: trigger peripheral reset or clear security state / rst_1..rst_13 and security/lock state affected by reset, including DMA rst_8 / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: NoC/software reset requester Operation: trigger peripheral reset or clear security state Object: rst_1..rst_13 and security/lock state affected by reset, including DMA rst_8 Expected guard: Software/NoC-controlled resets should require privilege/access-control/lifecycle authorization and should not clear lock/security policy state unless explicitly permitted. Observed behavior: Top-level declares reset lines rst_1..rst_13 and exposes reset NoC ports. DMA wrapper receives rst_8 and uses reset condition ~(rst_ni && ~rst_8) to clear core_lock_reg/start/length/source/dest registers. The reset-control wrapper/decode and acc_ctrl gate were not provided, so authorization for asserting rst_N and whether rst_6/rst_9 clear acc_ctrl/reglk_ctrl cannot be determined. Path: buf_ariane_rst_noc2_* -> reset NoC/AXI-lite path -> reset wrapper/control registers -> rst_1..rst_13 -> peripheral wrappers including DMA rst_8 Closure verdict: Related RTL evidence exists, but some core evidence slots remain incomplete.
findings.security_impact: If reset control is not privileged or can reset lock/policy state, software may bypass locks by resetting protected peripherals such as DMA or policy wrappers.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Reset wrapper source/decode, access-control gate for reset writes, and reset effects on acc_ctrl/reglk_ctrl.

findings.summary: Visible debug/test signals cross into protected policy/security assets. jtag_unlock is not shown gating debug entry but is supplied to reglk_wrapper; debug_mode_i is passed to crypto wrappers. These paths are not proven confined to lifecycle/simulation modes and may bypass normal acc_ctrl/reglk checks depending on wrapper internals.
findings.vulnerability_category: read/write/override protected state through debug or test path / protected peripheral state, DMA configuration, fuse/crypto data, acc_ctrl/reglk_ctrl / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: debug/JTAG/test requester Operation: read/write/override protected state through debug or test path Object: protected peripheral state, DMA configuration, fuse/crypto data, acc_ctrl/reglk_ctrl Expected guard: testmode_i, SimDTM/SimJTAG, debug_mode_i, and jtag_unlock paths should not bypass normal acc_ctrl/reglk/PMP checks unless simulation-only or lifecycle-authorized. Observed behavior: dmi_jtag receives testmode_i and drives debug_req/debug_req_valid toward dm_top without visible top-level jtag_unlock gate. SimDTM/SimJTAG branches can drive debug request paths under preprocessor conditions. reglk_wrapper receives jtag_unlock, and AES/RSA wrappers receive debug_mode_i, but the provided evidence does not show that these debug/test signals are restricted to authorized lifecycle state or that any writes use normal access-control/lock gates. Path: JTAG/testmode/SimDTM/SimJTAG -> debug_req/debug_req_valid/dm_top and jtag_unlock/debug_mode_i -> reglk/security wrappers -> protected policy/security state Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Debug/test modes may provide alternate authorization or bypass channels to policy locks and secret-capable peripherals if wrappers interpret jtag_unlock/debug_mode_i/we_flag as override signals without lifecycle confinement.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Wrapper internals for reglk/security peripherals and preprocessor/lifecycle build constraints.

global_uncertainty: No closure-level missing evidence was recorded beyond the visible source scope limitations.
